section.title: More vocabulary and notation
section.p: We say that sets A,B are equipotent, denoted A\sim B, if they have the same cardinality. By Theorem 7.1.3 in , equipotence is an equivalence relation. Given a set A, we write |A|, pronounced the cardinality of the set A, to denote the equivalence class of A with respect to \sim. Note that this usage is consistent with the notation |S|=n for a finite set S of size n, where n is a nonnegative integer.

exercise.p: Explain what it means to say that the notation |A| for the equipotence class of the set A is consistent with the notation for the size of a finite set.

theorem.title: (Schroeder-Bernstein)
theorem.statement: If f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to A are one-to-one maps, then A\sim B.

ol.li: Notation: Because g is one-to-one, but not necessarily onto, the preimage set g^{-1}(\{b\}) of the one-element set \{b\}\subseteq B is either empty, or contains exactly one element. We will adopt a slight abuse of notation and write g^{-1}(b) to denote either the empty set, or the single element of g^{-1}(\{b\}), respectively. (That is, we will write g^{-1}(b_0)=a_0 to mean that g^{-1}(\{b_0\})=\{a_0\}.) We will use the same convention for f^{-1}(a) for a\in A.
For each a\in A, let \Ances(a) denote the sequence a,g^{-1}(a),f^{-1}(g^{-1}(a)),g^{-1}(f^{-1}(g^{-1}(a))),\ldots .

Each entry of \Ances(a) is either an element of A, an element of B, or is the empty set. If any entry of \Ances(a) is the empty set, then every entry past that point is also the empty set. If \Ances(a) has an entry that is the empty set, then we will write \Term(a) to denote the last entry (that is, the entry furthest to the right) in \Ances(a) that is not the empty set. If no entry in \Ances(a) is the empty set, we will say that \Term(a) does not exist.
Now show h is a bijection.

exercise.p:
Using the notation above, give examples of A,B,f,g,a such that
\Term(a)\in B
\Term(a)\in A
\Term(a) does not exist.

aside.title: Instructor solution for
aside.p:
Let A=\N_A, B=\N_B (that is A,B are distinguishable copies of \N) and let f,g both be the right shift n\to n+1. Then \Term(2_A)=1_B.
Ditto above. \Term(3_A)=\Term(1_A)=1_A.
Let A=B=\{\ast\} and let f,g both be the identity function.
Note 4/2026: let's collect more examples and expand this exercise.

exercise.p:
Let A=[0,1], B=(0,1), and let f\colon A\to B and g\colon B\to A both be given by the formula x\to \frac{2x+1}{4}. Using the notation above, find the following.
\Ances(0)
\Ances(5/16)
h(0)
h(5/16)

aside.title: Instructor solution for
aside.p:
\Ances(0)=(0,\emptyset,\emptyset,\emptyset,\ldots)
\Ances(5/16)=(5/16,1/8,\emptyset,\emptyset,\ldots)
h(0)=f(0)=1/4
h(5/16)=g^{-1}(5/16)=1/8
Note 4/2026: let's add more parts to this exercise. For example, find the set of all the elements of A whose terminator is in B.

exercise.p:
Complete the proof of the Schroeder-Bernstein Theorem following the outline given above. Address each of the following questions.
In step 1, why is writing g^{-1}(b) called abuse of notation?
Justify the claim made in the first two sentences of step 3.
Suppose that \Term(a)\in B. Explain why it must be that g^{-1}(a) is not the empty set. Say why this is needed for step 4.
Complete step 5.

ol.li: The issue is that the functions f^{-1},g^{-1} may or may not exist. In NJC, we allow writing f^{-1}(t_0) to denote f^{-1}(\{t_0\}), but this is taken to denote a subset of the domain, not a single element of the domain. The notational abuse given in this proof goes one step further.

The first element of the sequence \Ances(a) is in a\in A. If \Term(a)\in B, then there are at least two entries in \Ances(a) that are not the empty set. This guarantees that g^{-1}(a) exists. We need this in order to define h.
(explain, show h is one-to-one and onto)
